author: @derekcartledge3057
text: If you run out of heavy duty hand cleaner put cooking oil on your hand and grind for three or four minutes then wash off with fairy liquid the cooking oil helps dissolve The engine oil add your sugar or salt to improve it does work quite well try it and let me know what you thinkCheers Derek

author: @christianfrandfors6258
text: If your unlucky the sugar can eventually clog your drain, that is if you don't rinse with enough water. It can turn in to a big lollipop. That never happens with salt and salt is good for your skin. But then, on the other hand, if you ever have the urge for a lollipop at 2pm just unscrew your drain and problem solved ...

author: @satibel
text:
I personally use flour to get my hands soft.
Also tip to scrub tubes/ bottles: pour table salt that has some grit in it, and shake, than you can just clean it with water.

author: @thomashardin911
text: U ROCK! 💯💃🏻🎸

author: @edwardcdg
text: We have a hand soap over here called 'Lava'.

author: @dwietr
text: Nice, I've could have sworn by a salt and (olive) oil mixture (followed by a soap wash) to remove grease and glue as a diy method. I never knew you could just as easily use sugar and soap, that saves a step.

text: but is it safe for diabetics?with all that sugar LOLOLOL

text:
Swarfega is the stuff mechanics use!!!

author: @andrewlast9960
text: good old dirt and soap if you have or just dirt and water will clean your hand very well. I discovered that trick working remote site much cheaper than sugar , keep the sugar for the coffee and tea . cheers

author: @donwright3427
text: If you want to make that green jelly hand cleaner simply mix dish soap and turps

author: @craigglewis
text: Butter and sugar was an old-time one. Always worked :)   Spot of grease on your chin  ;) lol

text: Fix those faucets

author: @tommyschauer3093
text: GOOD VID.  GOOD WORK.

author: @lightcapmath2777
text: Salt is cheaper than sugar...no? DVD:)

author: @docink6175
text: I learned decades ago that the best way to get greasy grime off your hands is with fresh grease or oil, I was hand packing bearings and my hands were super clean afterwards, either fresh grease or oil will float the grime right out of your pores and from under your nails, then you wip it off and use a dish detergent that cuts grease to finish up.. and it doesnt need to be motor oil, cheap cooking oil works just as well

replies.author: @DeathValleyDazed
replies.text: Hey, thanks for your slick tip!

author: @weegaz22
text: laundry washing powder has the same effect, little bit of fairy liquid, rub it into your hands then add the washing powder, Its the mechanics home version of swarfega.

author: @StrumHollow
text: Don't be using that sugar in your tea after dipping those hands in it ;)

author: @lebeyes
text: The old scrubing soap had sand in it, cheaper than sugar. The new stuff has microplastics, not good :-(

author: @Web3Dre
text: You missed a spot 😄

author: @tenij000
text: shampoo works to

author: @w8lvradio
text: Salt works too. You can also use TOOTHPASTE.

author: @sc20910
text: Why aren’t faucets there giving a combined hot/cold single output so you can alter the temp instead of just using all hot or all cold

replies.author: @w8lvradio
replies.text: They usually have seperate in the UK.

replies.author: @DaymeEdna
replies.text: Having separate taps for hot and cold water was, in the past, mandated in the UK to prevent domestic hot water storage units from contaminating the fresh (cold) water supply. I believe that the separation no longer required, but people stick with it because that is the system that they grew up with and are comfortable with. It drives me insane whenever I’m in the UK.

author: @babaone23
text: All these fantastic project builds and you have a wonky hot tap.. tut tut - haha - love it

author: @Thereisnosp00000n
text: Being a bike mechanic drinking a lot of coffee I dry the old grounds on newspaper and use it as a scrub. The oils left in the grounds help loosen up grime and it smells great. And it's made of something that otherwise would've been thrown in the trash.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is awesome!! Talk about reuse, I really like this, Thanks mate! :)

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Must get my grandchild to watch this. His hands after he has washed them look like Luke’s hands before he washed them. They still look mucky after his cleaned them further on the towel.

author: @AM-us7cz
text: Luke the hot water tap handel, don't forget to clean it.  Well done.

author: @roberthopgood1894
text: liquid dish soap does a very good job with greasy hands

author: @arb261268
text:
Learnt from this from my dad 40+ years ago, he was a tool maker for an engineering company and they used this technique when they ran out of swarfega (which was probably what you used to use). 

I also learnt a little while back that if you have sticky gunk on your hands then use olive oil which removes it beautifully.

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: When painting a little cooking oil on your hands before makes it so the paint won't stick to you.

author: @ianbutler1983
text: Excellent Luke! Here is the US they sell a soap called Lava that has pumice in it. I never thought of using sugar.

author: @prevengeix8551
text: I find borax is a great and inexpensive alternative and leaves hands super soft. Also anything with pumice.
